questions.stem: What does the Same-Origin Policy primarily prevent?

choices.text: One website from reading or tampering with data belonging to another website with a different origin.
Servers from logging client IP addresses.
Users from visiting more than one website per session.
The use of cookies entirely.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 7: SOP is enforced by the browser to isolate origins. A script in one page may access data in a second page only if both pages share an origin.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Domain** — Server-side logging is unrelated to SOP.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concept** — SOP governs cross-origin script access, not navigation.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Mechanism** — SOP and cookie policy are separate policies.

questions.stem: Two URLs share the same origin if and only if which fields match exactly (string-equal)?

choices.text: Protocol, domain, and path.
Protocol, domain, and port.
Domain only.
Protocol and port only.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 9: 'protocol, domain, and port strings must be equal.' Path and query are NOT part of origin.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Triple** — Path is not part of the origin.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Too Loose** — Subdomain mismatches break origin equality (cci.charlotte.edu vs charlotte.edu).
distractorAnalysis.D: **Missing Domain** — Domain mismatch (a.com vs b.com) clearly breaks origin.

questions.stem: If `https://test.com` includes `<script src="https://google.com/lib.js">`, what origin does the loaded JavaScript run with?

choices.text: https://google.com — the origin of the script's URL.
https://test.com — the origin of the page that loaded it (test.com effectively 'copy-pasted' the JS into its page).
about:blank — scripts have no origin.
The intersection of both origins; neither's data is accessible.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 10: cross-origin script files run with the *embedding* page's origin. This is exactly why third-party JS inclusion is so dangerous — the included script gets full access to the embedder's data.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Common Misconception** — Intuitive but wrong; SOP makes scripts adopt the embedder's origin, not the source URL's.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Executing JS always has an associated origin.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Fabrication** — There is no 'intersection' rule; origin is single-valued.

questions.stem: Which of the following is *NOT* a SOP exception listed on slide 10?

choices.text: Websites can fetch and display images from other origins (without manipulating pixel data).
CORS lets two origins agree on limited sharing.
The postMessage JS API allows controlled cross-origin messaging.
JavaScript may freely read cookies from any origin if requested politely.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: SOP and cookie policy explicitly forbid cross-origin cookie reads. The other three are real, documented exceptions on slide 10.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Real Exception** — Image loading is allowed; pixel access is not.
distractorAnalysis.B: **Real Exception** — CORS is the canonical opt-in cross-origin sharing mechanism.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Real Exception** — postMessage is the documented JS bridge between origins.

questions.stem: What is a cookie?

choices.text: A piece of data used to maintain state across multiple HTTP requests, set by server or browser and stored client-side.
A type of HTTP method like GET or POST.
A server-side database row indexed by user ID.
An encrypted hash of a session.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 14: cookies are name–value pairs the browser stores and automatically attaches on relevant requests, used by servers to customize responses and connect related requests across HTTP's stateless model.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Layer** — Cookies are headers, not methods.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Storage** — Cookies live in the browser, not the server's database.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Format** — Cookies are arbitrary strings; encryption is not required.

questions.stem: If a cookie has `Secure=True`, what does the browser do?

choices.text: Encrypts the cookie's value at rest.
Only sends the cookie over HTTPS connections; plain HTTP requests will not include it.
Hides the cookie from the user in DevTools.
Signs the cookie with a server-issued key.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 17: Secure restricts *transmission* to HTTPS. The cookie value at rest in the browser is unchanged; this flag governs the channel.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Action** — Secure does not encrypt the cookie at rest.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — DevTools still shows Secure cookies.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Fabrication** — No automatic signing is implied.

questions.stem: What does `HttpOnly=True` on a cookie prevent?

choices.text: Sending the cookie over HTTPS.
JavaScript running in the browser from reading or modifying the cookie via document.cookie.
The server from setting the cookie.
The cookie from being sent on POST requests.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 17: HttpOnly stops in-page JS from accessing the cookie — a key defense against session-token theft via XSS.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Flag** — That would be the inverse of Secure; HttpOnly is unrelated to HTTPS.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Servers freely set HttpOnly cookies.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Fabrication** — HttpOnly does not restrict by HTTP method.

questions.stem: Server `mail.google.com` issues a Set-Cookie header with `Domain=com`. What happens?

choices.text: Allowed — `com` is a valid suffix of the server's domain.
Rejected — `com` is a top-level domain (TLD); cookie policy forbids setting cookies on TLDs.
Allowed — but the cookie expires immediately.
Allowed — but only for HTTPS requests.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 24: Y must be a domain suffix of X *and* not a TLD. Without the TLD ban, mail.google.com could plant cookies that the browser would attach to *every* .com site — catastrophic cross-site contamination.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Suffix Trap** — Suffix-match alone is insufficient; the TLD ban is the second rule.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Failure Mode** — Expiry is not the issue.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Flag** — Secure flag is independent.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a *valid* Set-Cookie under cookie policy?

choices.text: Server `mail.google.com` sets `Domain=google.com`.
Server `evil.com` sets `Domain=google.com`.
Server `google.com` sets `Domain=com`.
Server `mail.google.com` sets `Domain=mail.evil.com`.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Y=google.com is a domain suffix of X=mail.google.com, and google.com is not a TLD — both rules of slide 24 are satisfied.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Suffix Violation** — evil.com is not a suffix of google.com.
distractorAnalysis.C: **TLD Violation** — com is a TLD; rule violated.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Suffix Violation** — mail.evil.com is not a suffix of mail.google.com.

questions.stem: A cookie has `Domain=charlotte.edu`, `Path=/academics`. Will the browser send it on a request to `https://cci.charlotte.edu/academics/sis-faculty`?

choices.text: Yes — `charlotte.edu` is a domain suffix of `cci.charlotte.edu` and `/academics` is a path prefix of the request path.
No — the request URL must exactly match the cookie's Domain+Path.
No — the cookie's Domain is broader than the server's.
Only over HTTPS, regardless of suffix/prefix.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slides 25–26: cookie Domain must be a *suffix* of the server domain (✓ charlotte.edu suffix of cci.charlotte.edu) AND cookie Path must be a *prefix* of the server path (✓ /academics prefix of /academics/sis-faculty).

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Rule** — Suffix/prefix matching is the rule, not exact match.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Reversed** — A broader Domain is precisely what suffix-match allows.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Different Flag** — Secure is not specified in this scenario.

questions.stem: What is a session token?

choices.text: A user's hashed password sent on every request.
A randomly generated secret value the server issues after authentication; the browser attaches it on subsequent requests so the server can identify the user without re-prompting credentials.
The user's IP address used as identity.
A public key signed by the server.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 30: session tokens replace per-request password entry. Servers must generate them randomly and securely; browsers must protect them via cookie policy + SOP.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Mechanism** — Sending a password on every request would be insecure and unnecessary.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Identity Model** — IP-based identity is unreliable and not session-token semantics.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Mechanism** — Public-key authentication is a different scheme.

questions.stem: Slide 33: which combination of attributes is *recommended* for a session-token cookie?

choices.text: `Secure=False`, `HttpOnly=False`, `Expires=never`.
`Secure=True`, `HttpOnly=True`, `Expires=session-timeout`, with Domain/Path scoped to authenticated endpoints.
`Domain=com`, `Secure=False`.
No attributes at all; rely on defaults.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Secure stops over-the-wire theft on HTTP; HttpOnly stops XSS-based theft; tight Domain/Path limits exposure surface; short expiry shrinks the theft window.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Disables Defenses** — Both major theft defenses turned off.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Illegal Domain** — Domain=com is forbidden (TLD).
distractorAnalysis.D: **Insecure Defaults** — Defaults invite session theft.

questions.stem: What is the core idea behind a CSRF attack?

choices.text: The attacker steals the victim's password from a phishing page.
The attacker tricks the victim's browser into making an unintended authenticated request to a target server, which trusts it because the browser auto-attaches the session cookie.
The attacker installs malware on the victim's machine.
The attacker impersonates the server with a fake TLS certificate.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 36: CSRF exploits the browser's automatic cookie attachment. The attacker doesn't read the cookie — they cause a request that goes out *with* the cookie attached, and the server can't tell.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Attack** — CSRF doesn't require credential theft.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Class** — Malware-on-host is a different attack.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Class** — TLS impersonation is MITM/cert-spoofing, not CSRF.

questions.stem: Slide 42: how can an attacker cause a victim's browser to issue a CSRF *GET* request without any user click?

choices.text: Embed `` on a page the victim visits — the browser auto-fetches the URL with bank.com's cookies attached.
Send the victim a physical letter.
Run a SQL injection against the victim's browser.
Force the victim to disable JavaScript.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The browser auto-fetches every img URL and attaches cookies for that domain. The image will fail to render, but the GET hits the bank server with the victim's session cookie still attached.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Channel** — Out-of-band social engineering isn't browser-level CSRF.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Target** — SQL injection targets databases, not browsers.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Backwards** — Disabling JS doesn't help an attacker who needs the browser to send the request.

questions.stem: Why can a simple `<a href=...>` link not directly forge a CSRF *POST* request?

choices.text: Clicking a link issues a GET, not a POST. To forge a POST, the attacker typically needs JavaScript or a hidden auto-submitting form on a page the victim loads.
Browsers refuse to follow links with form parameters.
POST requests cannot carry cookies.
The Same-Origin Policy blocks all POST requests.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 43: link clicks are GET-only. To produce a POST automatically, the attacker hosts a page that runs JS or auto-submits a form, which the victim's browser executes when they visit.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Fabrication** — Browsers happily follow links with query strings.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — POSTs include cookies just like GETs.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Scope** — SOP allows form submissions cross-origin (slide 44 explicitly notes this is why SOP alone doesn't stop CSRF).

questions.stem: Slide 49 says CSRF tokens *cannot* be sent to the server in a cookie. Why?

choices.text: Cookies are stateless and cannot carry tokens.
The browser would auto-attach the CSRF cookie to *every* request to that domain — including the attacker's forged request — defeating the defense entirely.
Cookies are encrypted at the boundary and the server cannot read them.
Cookie values cannot exceed 8 bytes.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The whole point of a CSRF token is to be *something the attacker can't include*. If it auto-attaches via cookies, the attacker gets it for free. Tokens must travel in headers, query parameters, or POST body — places SOP prevents attacker.com's JS from reading or writing on the victim's behalf.

distractorAnalysis.A: **False** — Cookies routinely carry tokens (session tokens *are* tokens).
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Cookies aren't encrypted at the server boundary.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Fabrication** — No 8-byte limit exists.

questions.stem: How does a CSRF token defend against a forged form POST?

choices.text: The legitimate server includes a per-session/per-form secret as a hidden field; the attacker's JS on evil.com cannot read the victim's HTML (SOP), so it cannot embed a valid token in its forged form.
The token encrypts the form payload.
The token rate-limits requests from the user.
The token is the user's password sent in a different field.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The attacker can fetch their *own* token, but it's bound to the attacker's session, not the victim's. Without reading the victim's HTML (blocked by SOP), the attacker cannot embed a valid victim-token.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Mechanism** — CSRF tokens are not encryption keys.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Control** — Rate-limiting is a separate defense.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Bad Practice** — Passwords are not repurposed as CSRF tokens.

questions.stem: How does Referer-header validation defend against CSRF?

choices.text: The server checks that the Referer header matches an expected same-site URL and rejects requests with cross-site Referers.
The server logs the Referer for forensic purposes only — no rejection.
The server signs the Referer header before responding.
The server blocks the Referer header from leaving the browser.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 53: a request with Referer=evil.com hitting bank.com/transfer is clearly cross-site and can be refused. Same-site requests pass.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Not a Defense** — Logging without enforcement doesn't block the attack.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Direction** — Servers don't sign request headers.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Actor** — Browsers control Referer transmission, not servers.

questions.stem: Slide 54 lists practical issues with Referer-based CSRF defense. Which is *NOT* listed as an issue?

choices.text: The Referer header may leak private information (e.g., internal URL paths).
Firewalls or browser privacy settings may strip the header.
The header is optional; missing-header requests create an 'allow vs deny' dilemma.
The Referer header is encrypted by default and unreadable by the server.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The Referer is a plain HTTP header, not encrypted (beyond TLS in transit, which the server terminates). Slide 54 lists privacy leakage, header stripping, and the missing-header dilemma — but not encryption.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Listed** — Privacy leakage is the first issue on slide 54.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Listed** — The fail-safe-defaults dilemma is on slide 54.

questions.stem: What does `SameSite=Strict` on a cookie do?

choices.text: The browser will not send the cookie if the cookie's domain does not match the origin domain of the page making the request — blocking the cookie on cross-site requests.
It encrypts the cookie's value.
It restricts the cookie to a single browser tab.
It prevents the server from updating the cookie.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 55: `SameSite=Strict` makes the cookie a same-site-only cookie. evil.com's request to bank.com will not carry bank.com's session cookie — closing the CSRF channel for that cookie.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Scope** — Cookie scoping is not tab-bound.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Behavior** — Set-Cookie still works; SameSite governs *sending* on cross-site requests.

questions.stem: Slide 61: two cookies `name=value1; Domain=bank.com; Path=/` and `name=value2; Domain=bank.com; Path=/page` both apply to `bank.com/page`. What problem arises?

choices.text: The browser deletes both cookies on conflict.
The server receives both cookies in undefined order, and because Domain/Path attributes are *not* sent to the server, it cannot reliably distinguish them by their original scoping.
Only the most recently created cookie is sent.
The two cookies are merged into a single concatenated value.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The server sees `name=value1; name=value2` (or value2; value1). It receives no Domain/Path metadata, and the order is unspecified. Authoring two same-name cookies with different scopes is a footgun.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Behavior** — Browsers don't auto-delete on conflict.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Rule** — Recency is not the rule.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Behavior** — No automatic merging happens.

questions.stem: Spectre (slides 63–65) exploits which combination?

choices.text: Chrome's old per-tab process model + branch-prediction speculative execution + a microarchitectural side channel (cache/timing) to leak data across origins sharing one process.
A bug in TLS 1.3 handshake.
A misconfigured Referer header on a cross-site request.
A flaw in BLP's star-property.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: When evil.com and victim.com share an OS process (old Chrome model), only the JS engine separated them. Spectre uses speculation past a bounds check + a cache-timing side channel to leak victim-origin memory. Defense: site isolation (each origin in its own OS process).

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Vehicle** — TLS is not the channel.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Class** — Referer is a CSRF concept, not Spectre.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Domain** — BLP is unrelated to browser microarchitecture.

questions.stem: What is the deployed mitigation for Spectre in modern Chrome and Firefox?

choices.text: Disable cookies entirely.
*Site Isolation* — run each origin in its own OS process so cross-origin speculation cannot reach the other origin's memory; the OS sandbox now enforces the boundary.
Encrypt all JavaScript before execution.
Disable speculative execution at the OS level.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Slide 66: Chrome and Firefox now run each origin in its own process. The OS sandbox separates evil.com and victim.com; the speculative-execution channel cannot reach across processes without a much harder OS escape. Cost: memory and process-switch overhead.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Defense** — Disabling cookies doesn't address Spectre.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Not Deployable** — Encrypting JS isn't a real defense.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Too Costly** — Browsers don't disable speculation; they isolate origins instead.
